problems.title: Count Element

problems.starter_code:

    # Write your solution here.

problems.solution:

def main(tuple1):

    print(count)

if __name__ == "__main__":

    main(tuple1)

problems.title: Immutable Nature of Tuples
problems.description: Attempt to change the first element of the tuple to 100. Expected output: TypeError
problems.prompt: Attempt to change the first element of the tuple to 100. Expected output: TypeError
problems.starter_code:
import time

def main(tuple1, value, value_2, input_3, input_4):
    # Write your solution here.
    pass

if __name__ == "__main__":
    tuple1 = (1, 2, 3)

    value = 0

    value_2 = 100

    input_3 = 'Code: \n tuple1[0] = 100'

    input_4 = 'Output:\n'
    main(tuple1, value, value_2, input_3, input_4)
problems.solution:
import time

def main(tuple1, value, value_2, input_3, input_4):
    try:
        tuple1[value] = value_2
    except TypeError as e:
        print(input_3)
        print(input_4, e)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    tuple1 = (1, 2, 3)

    value = 0

    value_2 = 100

    input_3 = 'Code: \n tuple1[0] = 100'

    input_4 = 'Output:\n'
    main(tuple1, value, value_2, input_3, input_4)

examples.input:
tuple1 = (1, 2, 3)

value = 0

value_2 = 100

input_3 = 'Code: \n tuple1[0] = 100'

input_4 = 'Output:\n'
examples.output:
Code: 
 tuple1[0] = 100
Output:
 'tuple' object does not support item assignment

test_cases.input:

value = 0

value_2 = 100

input_3 = 'Code: \n tuple1[0] = 100'

input_4 = 'Output:\n'

Output:

test_cases.input:

value = 2
value_2 = 102
input_3 = 'Eqfg: \n vwrng3[2] = 322'
input_4 = 'Qwvrwv:\n'

Rxwsxw:

problems.title: List vs Tuple - Performance Comparison
problems.solution:
import time

def main():
    print('Measure the time taken to access all elements in a large list and a large tuple. Expected outcome: Faster access in tuple.')

    large_list = list(range(1000000))

    large_tuple = tuple(range(1000000))

    def access_elements(collection):
        for element in collection:
            _ = element

    start_time = time.time()

    access_elements(large_list)

    list_time = time.time() - start_time

    print('List Access Time:\n {:.6f} seconds'.format(list_time))

    start_time = time.time()

    access_elements(large_tuple)

    tuple_time = time.time() - start_time

    print('Tuple Access Time:\n {:.6f} seconds'.format(tuple_time))

    print('-' * 20)

    print()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
notes: Measure the time taken to access all elements in a large list and a large tuple. Expected outcome: Faster access in tuple.
Stored as a standalone snippet because the block is not a simple hidden-setup exercise.

problems.title: List vs Tuple - Data Integrity and Mutability
problems.description: List vs Tuple - Data Integrity and Mutability
problems.prompt: List vs Tuple - Data Integrity and Mutability
notes: Compare the use of a list for mutable data and a tuple for immutable data.
Manage an inventory of items where quantities change over time.
Store fixed geographical coordinates that should not change.
